section: NVIDIA NeMo Platform — RAG Knowledge Base
content:
Source: [NVIDIA NeMo Platform Official Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/nemo-framework/user-guide/latest/index.html) 
Category: Model Customization, Evaluation, Guardrails, Deployment 
Tool Identity: NeMo is NVIDIA's end-to-end platform for building, fine-tuning, evaluating, and deploying AI models with enterprise-grade controls.

NeMo Platform is NVIDIA's infrastructure layer for building and deploying specialized AI agents using open-source models. It is not a single model — it is a full platform that handles the entire AI development lifecycle:

- Synthetic data generation — create training data at scale
- Model fine-tuning — customize models for your domain
- Evaluation — benchmark and measure model quality
- Security testing — scan for vulnerabilities via NeMo Auditor
- Guardrails — real-time content moderation and safety
- Inference — serve models through a unified gateway

Deployment options: Docker (local) or Kubernetes (production) 
Enterprise features: RBAC (Role-Based Access Control), observability, audit logging

Use NeMo when you need to:

NeMo supports the following use case: Fine-tune an LLM on domain-specific data. This is handled by Customizer + Data Designer.
NeMo supports the following use case: Evaluate model quality (accuracy, safety, RAG metrics). This is handled by Evaluator.
NeMo supports the following use case: Generate synthetic training data. This is handled by Data Designer Service.
NeMo supports the following use case: Add safety filters to an LLM app. This is handled by Guardrails.
NeMo supports the following use case: Fine-tune embedding models for RAG. This is handled by Customizer + Evaluator.
NeMo supports the following use case: Manage AI resources with RBAC across teams. This is handled by Workspaces + Roles.
NeMo supports the following use case: Scan AI agents for vulnerabilities. This is handled by NeMo Auditor.

NeMo vs Triton vs TensorRT — Quick Rule:
- Need to train or fine-tune a model → NeMo
- Need to serve/deploy a model at scale → Triton
- Need to optimize inference speed on GPU → TensorRT
- Full pipeline: NeMo → TensorRT → Triton (this is the standard NVIDIA production stack)

Workspaces are the fundamental organizational boundary in NeMo. Every resource (models, datasets, jobs, deployments) must belong to a workspace.

Role levels:
- Viewer — read-only access
- Editor — can create and modify resources
- Admin — full control including role management

Workspace naming rules:
- Must start with a lowercase letter (a–z)
- 2–63 characters long
- Allowed: lowercase letters, digits, hyphens
- No consecutive hyphens (--)
- Cannot end with a hyphen
- Cannot be renamed after creation — choose carefully

Built-in workspaces:
- default — general experimentation, editable by all authenticated users
- system — platform-provided resources (read-only for regular users)

Best practice: Use separate workspaces for true isolation (e.g., team-ml-research, env-production, client-acme). For lightweight grouping within a workspace, use Projects.

Entities are the underlying data objects for all NeMo resources. Every model, dataset, job, and config is stored as an entity.

- All entities share common metadata: name, workspace, timestamps, custom fields
- Entity names are unique within a workspace (same name can exist in different workspaces)
- Accessed via service-specific APIs (Customizer, Evaluator, Models) — not a generic entity API

NeMo provides encrypted secret storage for API keys (e.g., Hugging Face tokens, Weights & Biases keys).

- Secrets are encrypted at rest
- Once created, the secret value cannot be retrieved through the API — not even by Admins
- Only Platform Administrators can retrieve secret values
- Referenced in configs as workspace/secret_name

A Fileset is a named container for files, used for datasets, model artifacts, and evaluation results.

Supported storage backends:

NeMo — Backend: local; Type: Read/Write; Description: Default local filesystem storage.
NeMo — Backend: s3; Type: Read/Write; Description: Amazon S3 or S3-compatible (e.g., MinIO).
NeMo — Backend: ngc; Type: Read-Only; Description: NVIDIA GPU Cloud storage.
NeMo — Backend: huggingface; Type: Read-Only; Description: HuggingFace Hub repositories.

ModelDeploymentConfig — A versioned blueprint for deploying a NIM (NVIDIA Inference Microservice) container. Specifies:
- GPU count
- Container image
- Model name
- Optional: LoRA support, chat templates, tool calling, custom environment variables
- Reusable: multiple deployments from one config; updates create a new version without affecting existing deployments

ModelDeployment — A running instance of a NIM container. 
Lifecycle states: CREATED → PENDING → READY | FAILED
When READY, a ModelProvider is automatically created.

ModelProvider — A routable inference host. All inference requests are routed through a ModelProvider, which can serve one or more Models.

Model — A registered model entity (e.g., nvidia/NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B-BF16). Can be:
- Hosted locally via NIM
- External (NVIDIA Build, OpenAI, etc.)

Purpose: Apply safety checks and content moderation to LLM applications.

How it works:
1. User request arrives at the Guardrails OpenAI-compatible endpoint
2. Guardrails evaluates the input against configured policies
3. If safe, request is forwarded to the inference model
4. Model output is evaluated before returning to the user
5. Blocked content is handled according to the guardrail config

Use cases:
- Content safety (toxicity, hate speech)
- Topic control (keep models on-topic)
- PII protection
- Jailbreak detection

Key distinction: Guardrails is about protecting an LLM application in production. NeMo Auditor is about scanning AI agents for vulnerabilities before deployment.

NeMo — Primitive: Metrics; Purpose: Flexible scoring logic for model outputs; When to Use: Custom datasets, task-specific criteria.
NeMo — Primitive: Benchmarks; Purpose: Metrics + Dataset paired together; When to Use: Standardized comparisons, regression testing.

- Offline evaluation — score pre-existing model outputs (already generated)
- Online evaluation — generate outputs from a model and score them in one pipeline

Purpose: Orchestrate complex synthetic data generation workflows at scale.

Core capability: Coordinates LLM calls, manages dependencies between data fields, handles batching and parallelization, and validates generated data against specifications.

Typical workflow:
NeMo Studio is the web UI for NeMo Platform — a user-friendly interface that surfaces all NeMo capabilities visually. It is built on top of the same APIs available via CLI and Python SDK.

section: 9. NeMo in the Broader NVIDIA Stack
content:
NeMo handles the left side of the pipeline (data → training → evaluation → guardrails). 
Triton + TensorRT handle the right side (optimization → serving → monitoring).

NeMo — Action: List workspaces; Endpoint / Method: GET /v2/workspaces.
NeMo — Action: Create workspace; Endpoint / Method: POST /v2/workspaces.
NeMo — Action: Live metric evaluation; Endpoint / Method: POST /v2/workspaces/{workspace}/evaluation/metric-evaluate.
NeMo — Action: Submit metric job; Endpoint / Method: POST /v2/workspaces/{workspace}/evaluation/metric-jobs.
NeMo — Action: Submit benchmark job; Endpoint / Method: POST /v2/workspaces/{workspace}/evaluation/benchmark-jobs.
NeMo — Action: Register model provider; Endpoint / Method: POST /v2/workspaces/{workspace}/models/providers.

Common misconception about NeMo: ""NeMo is just a model"" — The correct understanding is: NeMo is a full platform — it includes data, training, eval, guardrails, and inference.
Common misconception about NeMo: ""I need NeMo to serve models"" — The correct understanding is: Triton is the primary serving layer; NeMo provides the gateway and management layer.
Common misconception about NeMo: ""Guardrails is optional for production"" — The correct understanding is: For enterprise deployments with user-facing LLMs, guardrails is a critical safety component.
Common misconception about NeMo: ""NeMo only works with NVIDIA models"" — The correct understanding is: NeMo supports open-source models from HuggingFace and external providers like OpenAI.

Last updated from official NVIDIA NeMo documentation. For the latest, see: https://docs.nvidia.com/nemo-framework/user-guide/latest/index.html

section: NVIDIA Triton Inference Server — RAG Knowledge Base
content:
Source: [NVIDIA Triton Inference Server Official Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/triton-inference-server/user-guide/docs/index.html) 
Category: Model Serving, Inference, Deployment 
Tool Identity: Triton is NVIDIA's open-source inference serving platform — it takes any trained/optimized model and exposes it as a production-grade HTTP/gRPC API.

NVIDIA Triton Inference Server is an open-source inference serving software that streamlines AI model deployment. It acts as the serving layer in the NVIDIA AI stack — taking models from frameworks like TensorRT, PyTorch, ONNX, and others, and exposing them as standardized API endpoints.

Core value proposition: Deploy any model, from any framework, to any hardware, behind a single, standardized API.

Part of: NVIDIA AI Enterprise — the production-grade AI software platform.

Use Triton when you need to:

Triton supports the following use case: Serve multiple models simultaneously. This is handled by Concurrent model execution.
Triton supports the following use case: Handle bursts of inference requests. This is handled by Dynamic batching.
Triton supports the following use case: Serve models across GPU, CPU, or edge devices. This is handled by Multi-hardware support.
Triton supports the following use case: Connect to Kubernetes / production infrastructure. This is handled by Health endpoints + metrics.
Triton supports the following use case: Build model pipelines (chained inference). This is handled by Ensembling + Business Logic Scripting.
Triton supports the following use case: Serve stateful models (e.g., speech, video). This is handled by Sequence batching.
Triton supports the following use case: Benchmark LLM throughput and latency. This is handled by GenAI-Perf tool.

Triton vs NeMo vs TensorRT — Quick Rule:
- Need to train or fine-tune a model → NeMo
- Need to serve/deploy a model with a standard API → Triton
- Need to optimize GPU inference speed → TensorRT (then serve with Triton)
- Common pattern: TensorRT optimizes the engine → Triton serves it → clients query via HTTP/gRPC

Triton supports a wide range of ML/DL backends out of the box:

The TensorRT backend in Triton supports NVIDIA's optimized inference engine.
The PyTorch (TorchScript) backend in Triton supports Meta's deep learning framework.
The ONNX Runtime backend in Triton supports Cross-framework open standard.
The TensorFlow backend in Triton supports Google's ML framework.
The OpenVINO backend in Triton supports Intel's inference optimization toolkit.
The Python backend in Triton supports Custom Python-based backends.
The RAPIDS FIL backend in Triton supports NVIDIA's ML inference for tree models.
The TensorRT-LLM backend in Triton supports Optimized LLM inference (via TRT-LLM backend).

Key insight: This multi-backend support is what differentiates Triton. You can serve a TensorRT-optimized LLM and a PyTorch vision model from the same Triton instance simultaneously.

Model Repository: A file-system-based directory where all model artifacts are stored. Triton reads from this on startup and can be dynamically updated via the Model Management API.

Request flow:
1. Inference request arrives via HTTP/REST, gRPC, or C API
2. Routed to the per-model scheduler
3. Scheduler batches requests (if configured)
4. Backend performs GPU/CPU inference
5. Outputs returned to the client

Triton can automatically group incoming requests into batches to maximize GPU utilization, even when clients send individual requests. This is critical for LLM serving where batching dramatically improves throughput.

For stateful models (speech recognition, video, multi-turn conversations), Triton maintains implicit state across a sequence of requests from the same client. The model doesn't need to re-receive the full context each time.
Multiple instances of the same model (or entirely different models) can run simultaneously on the same GPU or across multiple GPUs. Triton manages resource allocation automatically.

Chain multiple models together in a pipeline. For example:

Triton handles the data flow between models, eliminating the need for client-side orchestration.
For pipelines that require conditional logic (e.g., "if the first model's output exceeds a threshold, call model B"), BLS allows writing Python-based orchestration logic that runs inside Triton.
Triton's extensible architecture allows adding custom backends — custom pre/post-processing operations or entirely new inference frameworks — via the Backend C API.

section: Advanced LLM Configuration Options (via TRT-LLM backend):
content:
KV Cache: Reuse attention key-value pairs across requests to reduce memory bandwidth.
Chunked Context: Split long contexts into chunks; batch during generation for higher throughput.
Speculative Decoding: Use a small draft model to predict tokens; verify with large model for speedup.
MIG Support: Run multiple model instances on GPU partitions for isolation and efficiency.
Quantization: INT8/FP8/INT4 to reduce model size and increase throughput.
LoRA: Serve multiple fine-tuned adapters from a single base model.
Beam Search: For tasks requiring multiple candidate outputs (translation, code gen).

Triton runs on a wide range of hardware:

Triton supports NVIDIA GPUs (all modern) hardware (Full support, best performance).
Triton supports x86 CPU hardware (Supported via ONNX Runtime / OpenVINO).
Triton supports ARM CPU hardware (Supported (edge deployments)).
Triton supports AWS Inferentia hardware (Supported).
Triton supports NVIDIA SoCs (Jetson) hardware (Supported via C API and ARM builds).

Triton — Protocol: HTTP/REST; Use Case: Standard web clients, easy integration.
Triton — Protocol: gRPC; Use Case: High-performance, low-latency clients.
Triton — Protocol: C API; Use Case: Embedded/edge deployments, in-process use.
Triton — Protocol: Java API; Use Case: JVM-based applications.
Triton — Protocol: KServe Protocol; Use Case: Community standard for interoperability.

Triton provides production-grade observability out of the box:

- Readiness endpoint — Is Triton ready to serve? (for Kubernetes)
- Liveness endpoint — Is Triton alive? (for Kubernetes health checks)
- Metrics endpoint — Prometheus-compatible metrics:
 - GPU utilization
 - Server throughput (requests/sec)
 - Server latency (p50, p90, p99)
 - Queue time, compute time, transfer time
 - Per-model statistics

GenAI-Perf: Command-line tool for benchmarking LLM throughput and latency on Triton. Part of the Triton toolkit.

```bash

Triton integrates naturally with Kubernetes:
- Readiness/liveness probes → Kubernetes health checks
- Prometheus metrics → Grafana dashboards
- Horizontal Pod Autoscaler support
- Helm charts available via NGC
For resource-constrained environments (Jetson, embedded), use the C API or ARM builds. Triton can run in-process with no network overhead.

Triton exposes a dedicated API for managing models at runtime (via HTTP/REST, gRPC, or C API):

- Load/unload models without restarting Triton
- Query model status (is it ready, loading, unloading?)
- Get model metadata (input/output shapes, data types)
- Get server statistics

Common misconception about Triton: ""Triton only works with TensorRT"" — The correct understanding is: Triton supports 10+ backends including PyTorch, ONNX, Python, etc..
Common misconception about Triton: ""I need Triton for local development"" — The correct understanding is: Triton is for production serving; for dev, just use the framework's native inference.
Common misconception about Triton: ""Triton replaces TensorRT"" — The correct understanding is: Triton serves models; TensorRT optimizes them — complementary, not competing.
Common misconception about Triton: ""Triton only works on GPUs"" — The correct understanding is: Triton supports x86 CPU, ARM, AWS Inferentia, and edge devices.
Common misconception about Triton: ""I have to restart Triton to load a new model"" — The correct understanding is: The Model Management API allows hot-loading models at runtime.

Every model in Triton's repository needs a config.pbtxt:

Last updated from official NVIDIA Triton documentation. For the latest, see: https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/triton-inference-server/user-guide/docs/index.html

Source: [NVIDIA TensorRT Official Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/tensorrt/developer-guide/index.html) 
Category: Inference Optimization, GPU Acceleration, Model Compilation 
Tool Identity: TensorRT is NVIDIA's inference optimization SDK — it takes a trained model and produces a highly optimized, GPU-specific engine for maximum inference speed.

NVIDIA TensorRT is a high-performance deep learning inference SDK that optimizes trained models for deployment on NVIDIA GPUs. It is not a training framework — it is purely an optimization and runtime layer.

What TensorRT does:
1. Takes a trained model (from PyTorch, TensorFlow, ONNX, etc.)
2. Applies graph optimizations, layer fusions, kernel auto-tuning
3. Produces a serialized "engine" (a .plan file) optimized for a specific GPU
4. Runs inference with that engine at maximum throughput and minimum latency

Current version: TensorRT 10.x (TensorRT 11.0 coming soon with modernized APIs)

Use TensorRT when you need to:

Tensorrt supports the following use case: Maximize inference speed on NVIDIA GPU. This is handled by Kernel auto-tuning, layer fusion, graph optimization.
Tensorrt supports the following use case: Reduce model memory footprint. This is handled by INT8/FP8/FP4 quantization.
Tensorrt supports the following use case: Deploy production LLMs at scale. This is handled by TensorRT-LLM (TRT-LLM).
Tensorrt supports the following use case: Run models with mixed precision. This is handled by FP16/BF16/INT8 mixed precision.
Tensorrt supports the following use case: Support models with variable input sizes. This is handled by Dynamic Shapes.
Tensorrt supports the following use case: Partition GPU for multiple isolated workloads. This is handled by MIG (Multi-Instance GPU) support.

TensorRT vs Triton vs NeMo — Quick Rule:
- Need to optimize a model for GPU inference → TensorRT
- Need to serve a model with an API → Triton (which uses TensorRT as a backend)
- Need to train or fine-tune a model → NeMo
- The natural stack: NeMo trains → TensorRT optimizes → Triton serves

What the Builder does:
- Eliminates dead computations
- Folds constants
- Fuses layers (e.g., Conv + BN + ReLU → single kernel)
- Selects the fastest CUDA kernel for each operation
- Applies precision reduction (FP16, INT8) where safe
- Generates CUDA kernels tuned for the specific GPU

> ⚠️ Important: The build phase can take minutes to hours for large models. This is expected — it's a one-time cost.

Tensorrt supports the FP32 data type (32 bits), typically used for Default, maximum accuracy.
Tensorrt supports the TF32 data type (19 bits), typically used for Ampere GPUs, good accuracy/speed balance.
Tensorrt supports the FP16 data type (16 bits), typically used for Great speedup on tensor cores, minimal accuracy loss.
Tensorrt supports the BF16 data type (16 bits), typically used for Better dynamic range than FP16 for LLMs.
Tensorrt supports the FP8 data type (8 bits), typically used for Hopper GPUs, major speedup for LLMs.
Tensorrt supports the FP4 data type (4 bits), typically used for Blackwell GPUs, extreme compression.
Tensorrt supports the INT8 data type (8 bits), typically used for Legacy quantization, requires calibration.
Tensorrt supports the INT4 data type (4 bits), typically used for Weight-only quantization.
Tensorrt supports the INT32 data type (32 bits), typically used for Integer operations.
Tensorrt supports the BOOL data type (1 bits), typically used for Boolean logic.

In Tensorrt, Strong Typing mode is used for TensorRT statically infers types; no automatic precision changes.
In Tensorrt, Weak Typing mode is used for TensorRT may substitute lower precision for speed.

Best practice: Use strongly typed networks for better precision control, faster build times, and future compatibility.
By default, TensorRT optimizes for fixed input shapes. Dynamic Shapes allow the engine to handle variable input sizes at runtime.

Define one or more OptimizationProfile with:
- Minimum shape — smallest allowed input
- Optimal shape — shape TensorRT optimizes for
- Maximum shape — largest allowed input

TensorRT generates kernels valid across the entire [min, max] range, fastest at the optimal point.

TensorRT-LLM is a specialized, open-source library built on top of TensorRT specifically for large language models.

What TRT-LLM adds over vanilla TensorRT:
- LLM-specific optimizations: in-flight batching, paged KV cache, speculative decoding
- Pre-built support for popular LLM architectures (LLaMA, Mistral, GPT, Falcon, Gemma, etc.)
- Easy Python API for building TensorRT engines for LLMs
- Integration with Triton via the TRT-LLM backend

In-Flight Batching: Process requests mid-generation without waiting for batch completion.
Paged KV Cache: Efficient memory management for attention key-value pairs (like OS paging).
Speculative Decoding: Small model predicts; large model verifies — faster token generation.
Chunked Prefill: Split long context into chunks for better batching during prefill.
Multi-GPU Support: Tensor parallelism and pipeline parallelism across multiple GPUs.
LoRA Support: Serve multiple fine-tuned adapters from one base model at runtime.
Quantization: INT8, FP8, INT4 with NVIDIA Model Optimizer.

section: Step 1: Convert model to TensorRT engine
content:
python convertcheckpoint.py --modeldir llama-3-8b --output_dir ./ckpt

trtllm-build --checkpoint_dir ./ckpt \
 --output_dir ./engine \
 --gemm_plugin float16 \
 --gptattentionplugin float16 \
 --maxbatchsize 8 \
 --maxinputlen 2048 \
 --maxoutputlen 512

section: 8. Key Tools in the TensorRT Ecosystem
content:
In the Tensorrt ecosystem, trtexec is used for CLI tool: benchmark, build engines, generate timing caches.
In the Tensorrt ecosystem, Polygraphy is used for Debug, compare outputs across backends, constant folding.
In the Tensorrt ecosystem, ONNX-GraphSurgeon is used for Edit ONNX graphs: replace subgraphs, add plugins.
In the Tensorrt ecosystem, Model Optimizer is used for Quantization, pruning, distillation for TensorRT deployment.
In the Tensorrt ecosystem, Torch-TensorRT is used for Compile PyTorch models with TensorRT.
In the Tensorrt ecosystem, Nsight Systems is used for Profile TensorRT applications end-to-end.
In the Tensorrt ecosystem, GenAI-Perf is used for Benchmark LLMs served by Triton.

MIG (available on Ampere A100 and later) partitions a single GPU into smaller, isolated GPU instances.

Use with TensorRT:
- If your model has low GPU utilization, MIG can run multiple smaller models on isolated GPU slices
- Each MIG slice has dedicated compute and memory with quality-of-service guarantees
- No interference between workloads on different MIG slices
- Optimal partitioning is application-specific

TensorRT 10.x introduces multi-GPU inference support:

DistCollective: Distributed collective ops (AllReduce, AllGather, Broadcast) via NCCL.
Multi-device attention: Split KV sequence across GPUs with context parallelism.

This enables serving models too large for a single GPU.

TensorRT uses semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH):
- MAJOR changes → incompatible API/ABI changes
- MINOR changes → backward-compatible additions
- PATCH changes → backward-compatible bug fixes

Engine compatibility:
- By default, engines are tied to: OS + CPU architecture + GPU model + TensorRT version
- For forward compatibility: Use version compatibility settings in the builder
- Serialized engines are not compatible across TensorRT versions by default
- Calibration caches: Generally reusable within a major version

Deprecation policy:
- 12-month migration period after any API is deprecated
- C++ APIs marked with TRTDEPRECATEDAPI / TRT_DEPRECATED macros
- Python APIs issue DeprecationWarning at runtime

- TensorRT allocates device memory for timing kernel candidates
- Control via IBuilderConfig::setMemoryPoolLimit() (workspace size)
- At least two copies of weights in host memory during build

- Engine allocates device memory for model weights upon deserialization
- ExecutionContext uses two memory types:
 - Persistent memory — per-context state (e.g., convolution edge masks)
 - Enqueue memory — intermediate activations + scratch memory during inference
- Use ICudaEngine::getDeviceMemorySizeV2() to query required memory
- Weight streaming — for models larger than GPU memory, stream weights from host to device at inference time (latency tradeoff)

Performance tip: Enable CUDA lazy loading:

This significantly reduces peak GPU and host memory usage and speeds up TensorRT initialization.

TensorRT objects are not thread-safe by default. The safe concurrency pattern:

✅ Safe patterns:
- Different threads → different ExecutionContext (from the same engine)
- Non-modifying operations on an engine from multiple threads
- Creating contexts from an engine in multiple threads
- Deserializing engines in parallel

❌ Not safe:
- Sharing a single ExecutionContext across threads
- Using multiple builders on the same GPU (timing interference)

Tensorrt — Library: libnvinfer.so; Size: Full; Use Case: Default; all operator implementations.
Tensorrt — Library: libnvinfer_lean.so; Size: Smaller; Use Case: Run version-compatible engines only.
Tensorrt — Library: libnvinfer_dispatch.so; Size: Smallest; Use Case: Shim that loads lean runtime; max compatibility.

Python equivalents: tensorrt, tensorrtlean, tensorrtdispatch

Common misconception about Tensorrt: ""TensorRT works on any GPU"" — The correct understanding is: TensorRT requires NVIDIA GPUs; Turing (SM 7.5+) for best results.
Common misconception about Tensorrt: ""TensorRT engines are portable"" — The correct understanding is: By default, engines are GPU + TensorRT version specific.
Common misconception about Tensorrt: ""Weak typing is fine for new projects"" — The correct understanding is: Weak typing is deprecated in 10.12; use strong typing.
Common misconception about Tensorrt: ""TensorRT can train models"" — The correct understanding is: TensorRT is inference-only; use PyTorch/NeMo for training.
Common misconception about Tensorrt: ""Quantization always hurts accuracy"" — The correct understanding is: With proper calibration or QAT, INT8/FP8 accuracy loss is often negligible.
Common misconception about Tensorrt: ""TRT-LLM is the same as TensorRT"" — The correct understanding is: TRT-LLM is a higher-level library built on TensorRT specifically for LLMs.

Last updated from official NVIDIA TensorRT documentation. For the latest, see: https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/tensorrt/developer-guide/index.html
